food_name: Guatemala Antigua Medium Roast Coffee Beans

ontology.foodon_gap_note: FOODON_03301242 covers roasted coffee beans as a category; no origin-specific (Guatemala Antigua) code exists. Closest parent used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A00LC covers roasted coffee generically; no Guatemala Antigua single-origin code exists. Closest parent used.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: SNOMED CT 34325006 covers coffee as a substance; no roast-level or origin-specific code exists. Closest parent used.

serving.description: 1 serving brewed coffee (approx. 10g ground coffee per 240ml cup)
serving.natural_language_portion: A standard single serving is approximately 10 g of ground coffee used to brew one 240 ml cup; the 250 g retail bag yields approximately 25 servings.

nutrient_source.title: Coffee, brewed from grounds, prepared with tap water — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy #14209 (values scaled to per-100g dry ground coffee basis using standard 10g/240ml brew ratio and USDA roasted coffee bean entry #14201)
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service — FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrients reported per 100 g of roasted ground coffee (dry weight) using USDA FoodData Central entry for roasted coffee beans (FDC ID 171890, 'Coffee, dry powder, not reconstituted' / roasted ground). This is the closest approved-source match for dry roasted ground coffee. The product is sold as a 250 g bag of medium-roast whole/ground beans from the Guatemala Antigua region. Nutrient values reflect the dry roasted bean matrix, not brewed liquid. Energy and macros are dominated by the roasting process; the product is consumed as a beverage ingredient, not eaten directly.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of coffee and coffee-based beverages — University of Sydney GI Database and peer-reviewed literature (Atkinson et al., 2008; Foster-Powell et al., 2002)
gi_source.source_match_notes: Black coffee and roasted coffee beans have a negligible glycemic response. The University of Sydney GI Database and peer-reviewed literature (Atkinson FS et al., Diabetes Care 2008; Foster-Powell K et al., Am J Clin Nutr 2002) confirm that plain coffee has a GI effectively at or near zero due to minimal available carbohydrate in the brewed beverage. Per the zero-calorie beverage glycemic rule, glycemic_index is set to 1 (the lowest valid positive value) as this product contains no glycemic carbohydrates in the brewed serving. gi_category is 'low'. glycemic_load_per_serving is 0.0 since the brewed cup contains negligible available carbohydrate.

hypertension.key_factor: Low sodium content; caffeine may cause transient blood pressure elevation
hypertension.guidance: Roasted coffee beans are very low in sodium (13 mg per 100 g dry weight); however, individuals with hypertension should be aware that caffeine in brewed coffee can cause a transient, short-lived rise in blood pressure and should limit intake to 1–2 cups per day as per clinical guidelines.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Negligible glycemic load per brewed serving; chlorogenic acids may improve insulin sensitivity
type2_diabetes.guidance: Brewed Guatemala Antigua medium-roast coffee has a glycemic load of effectively zero per serving and epidemiological evidence suggests moderate unsweetened coffee consumption (3–4 cups/day) is associated with reduced Type 2 Diabetes risk, likely mediated by chlorogenic acids; individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should consume it without added sugar or high-fat creamers.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Low saturated fat; diterpenes (cafestol, kahweol) in unfiltered preparations may raise LDL
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Filtered brewed coffee from Guatemala Antigua beans is low in saturated fat and cholesterol; individuals with cardiovascular disease should use paper-filtered brewing methods to minimise exposure to diterpenes (cafestol and kahweol), which can raise LDL cholesterol when consumed in unfiltered preparations such as French press or boiled coffee.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Guatemala Antigua Medium Roast Coffee Beans is a single-origin specialty coffee product sourced from the Antigua region of Guatemala, one of the world's most celebrated coffee-growing areas. The Antigua valley sits at approximately 1,500 metres above sea level, surrounded by three volcanoes whose mineral-rich soils impart a distinctive terroir to the Arabica beans grown there. This product is sold as a 250 g retail bag of medium-roast whole beans or custom-ground coffee, available at Starbucks Singapore outlets. The medium roast level preserves the origin character of the bean while developing balanced body and sweetness. Tasting notes include lemon brightness, dark chocolate depth, and soft spice warmth, making it an elegant and refined cup. Nutritionally, the dry roasted beans are energy-dense (approximately 331 kcal per 100 g dry weight) and rich in dietary fibre (24.5 g/100 g), potassium (2,020 mg/100 g), niacin (16.2 mg/100 g), and phosphorus (383 mg/100 g). However, as a beverage ingredient consumed in 10 g portions per cup, the per-serving nutrient contribution is modest. The brewed cup is virtually calorie-free when consumed black. The glycemic index is effectively zero (set to 1 per convention) and the glycemic load per serving is 0.0, making it suitable for individuals managing blood glucose. Chlorogenic acids present in medium-roast coffee are associated with improved insulin sensitivity and reduced Type 2 Diabetes risk in epidemiological studies. Individuals with cardiovascular disease should use paper-filtered brewing to avoid diterpene exposure. Custom grind options are available in-store at Starbucks Singapore. The product carries a minimum 14-day best-before guarantee at point of purchase.

confidence_reason: Nutrient values are adapted from USDA FoodData Central roasted ground coffee (FDC ID 171890), which is the closest approved-source match for dry roasted coffee beans. No Guatemala Antigua single-origin nutrient profile exists in HPB Singapore Foodo, ICMR-NIN, or FAO/INFOODS SEA. The USDA entry is a reliable secondary global source for this Western/international product category. GI is estimated from University of Sydney GI Database and peer-reviewed literature for plain coffee. Confidence is medium because the exact product (Guatemala Antigua, Starbucks Singapore, 250 g) is not directly profiled in any approved primary source; values are adapted from the closest defensible approved-source entry.
notes: 1. This product is a retail packaged roasted coffee bean product (250 g bag), not a prepared dish. Nutrients are reported per 100 g of dry roasted ground coffee (dry weight basis), not per 100 g of brewed beverage. 2. trans_fat_g is 0.0 — confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central for roasted coffee beans; naturally zero by product composition. cholesterol_mg is 0.0 — naturally zero as coffee beans contain no dietary cholesterol. vitamin_c_mg is 0.0 — confirmed zero; roasting destroys ascorbic acid. vitamin_a_mcg_rae is 0.0 — confirmed zero per USDA source. vitamin_b12_mcg is 0.0 — naturally zero as this is a plant-derived product. folate_mcg_dfe is 0.0 — confirmed zero per USDA source for roasted coffee. vitamin_d_mcg is 0.0 — naturally zero; coffee beans contain no vitamin D. 3. glycemic_index is set to 1 (effectively zero) as this beverage ingredient contains no glycemic carbohydrates in a standard brewed serving; gi_category is 'low' per convention. glycemic_load_per_serving is 0.0 since the brewed cup contains negligible available carbohydrate. 4. Multilingual local names (Chinese, Malay, Tamil, Hindi) are not applicable as the canonical name is an internationally recognised Spanish/English geographic designation (Guatemala Antigua); however, alias translations are provided for retrieval purposes. 5. Ontology codes use closest_parent match type as no single-origin Guatemala Antigua coffee code exists in FoodOn, FoodEx2, or SNOMED CT. 6. The product is sold at Starbucks Singapore (One Fullerton) via field survey data at SGD 17.70 for a 250 g bag. 7. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, calcium_mg, iron_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are all sourced from USDA FoodData Central FDC ID 171890 (roasted coffee beans). No -1 sentinel values are used in this record as USDA FoodData Central provides values for all reported fields.
